content.text: A Microsoft Enterprise Agreement (EA) is a volume licensing contract between an organisation and Microsoft. Companies sign a 3-year agreement committing to a minimum annual Azure spend in exchange for discounted pricing, a dedicated Microsoft account team, and the ability to manage Azure at scale through a structured billing hierarchy. EA is aimed at organisations with 500 or more users or devices, though some agreements apply to smaller organisations.

The key financial mechanism in an EA is the Azure Prepayment (formerly called Azure Monetary Commitment): the organisation pays Microsoft upfront or in annual instalments for a committed amount of Azure consumption. Resources are drawn down against this prepayment, and any usage beyond the committed amount is billed at negotiated overage rates.

attrs.alt: EA Hierarchy: Enrollment to Departments to EA Accounts to Subscriptions

content.text: An EA organises Azure billing and administration through four levels.
The top-level EA contract — tied to a billing account
View all costs, manage departments and accounts, download invoices
Optional grouping of EA accounts (maps to org units like Engineering, Finance)
View department costs, create and manage EA accounts within the department
Container for subscriptions — tied to an Account Owner (a user identity)
Create Azure subscriptions under the account, view account costs
The Azure resource container and billing boundary
Departments are optional. Small organisations often skip the Department level and create EA Accounts directly under the Enrollment.

The Azure Prepayment is a pre-purchased credit balance applied against your Azure consumption. As resources run, charges draw down from the prepayment. When the balance reaches zero, additional charges are billed as overage at the rates defined in the EA contract.
Prepayment is applied in the order: credit balance first, then overage.
Some services are excluded from prepayment and always billed as overage (Marketplace third-party, some support plans).
Prepayment does not expire mid-term — unused balance rolls over within the 3-year term (exact policy varies by contract).
At EA renewal, a new prepayment commitment is negotiated based on the prior year's consumption.

# Check EA balance and credit usage via the Billing REST API
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
BILLING_ACCOUNT="<enrollment-billing-account-id>"

# Get enrollment balance summary
curl -s -X GET \
  "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/${BILLING_ACCOUNT}/billingPeriods?api-version=2018-03-01-preview" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" | python3 -m json.tool

# List billing periods (months) for cost analysis
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/${BILLING_ACCOUNT}/billingPeriods?api-version=2018-03-01-preview" \
  --query "value[0:3].{Name:name,Start:properties.billingPeriodStartDate,End:properties.billingPeriodEndDate}" \
  -o table
EA customers have two portals to work with, each covering different aspects of the agreement.
View enrollment balance, manage departments/accounts, download price sheets and invoices
Modern billing management, cost analysis, budgets

Microsoft is migrating EA customers from ea.azure.com to the Azure portal's Cost Management + Billing section. Most operations that previously required the EA portal are now available in the Azure portal.

Under an EA, subscriptions are created under EA Accounts. The Account Owner identity must have an Azure AD account in the associated tenant. Subscriptions can be created programmatically via the Subscription REST API or the Azure CLI.

# List EA enrollment accounts accessible to your identity
az billing enrollment-account list \
  --query "[].{Name:name,PrincipalName:principalName}" -o table

# Get a specific enrollment account
az billing enrollment-account show \
  --name "<enrollment-account-object-id>" -o json

# Create a new subscription under an EA enrollment account
ENROLLMENT_ACCOUNT="<enrollment-account-object-id>"
BILLING_ACCOUNT="<billing-account-name>"

az account create \
  --display-name "eng-prod-eastus" \
  --offer-type MS-AZR-0017P \
  --enrollment-account-object-id "$ENROLLMENT_ACCOUNT" \
  --billing-account-name "$BILLING_ACCOUNT"

# Monitor creation — it is asynchronous
# The response contains an Azure-AsyncOperation header with a URL to poll
# Alternatively, list subscriptions and wait for the new one to appear
az account list --query "[?name=='eng-prod-eastus']" -o table

# Move new subscription to the correct management group immediately
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name "mg-engineering" \
  --subscription "$(az account list --query "[?name=='eng-prod-eastus'].id" -o tsv)"
EA environments typically grow to hundreds of subscriptions. A consistent naming convention prevents confusion and makes automation easier.

# Recommended naming pattern: <org>-<workload>-<environment>-<region>
# Examples:
#   contoso-payments-prod-eastus
#   contoso-payments-staging-eastus
#   contoso-identity-prod-global
#   contoso-platform-shared-eastus

# Rename an existing subscription
az account subscription rename \
  --subscription-id "<subscription-id>" \
  --subscription-name "contoso-payments-prod-eastus"

# Tag subscriptions for cost attribution (requires Contributor on the subscription)
# Note: subscriptions cannot be tagged directly via az tag — use the REST API
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
curl -s -X PATCH \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "tags": {
      "Department": "Engineering",
      "Environment": "prod",
      "CostCentre": "CC-1234",
      "Owner": "platform-team@contoso.com"
    }
  }' | python3 -m json.tool

EA Enterprise Administrators can access cost data at every level of the hierarchy through the billing REST API and Azure Cost Management. Cost exports to storage accounts allow downstream BI tools to consume the data.

# Download the EA usage report for a billing period via REST
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
BILLING_ACCOUNT="<ea-billing-account-name>"
BILLING_PERIOD="202606"  # YYYYMM format

# Get the usage detail download URL
curl -s -X GET \
  "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/${BILLING_ACCOUNT}/billingPeriods/${BILLING_PERIOD}/providers/Microsoft.Consumption/usageDetails?\$top=100&api-version=2019-10-01" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" | python3 -m json.tool | head -80

# Create a recurring cost export for the EA enrollment scope
az costmanagement export create \
  --name "ea-monthly-export" \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/${BILLING_ACCOUNT}" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --recurrence Monthly \
  --recurrence-period from="2026-07-01" to="2027-06-30" \
  --storage-account-id "/subscriptions/<finops-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-finops/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/finopsstorage" \
  --storage-container "ea-exports" \
  --storage-directory "azure/enrollment"

# Cost breakdown by department via Resource Graph (cross-subscription)
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/${BILLING_ACCOUNT}" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity None \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"Department"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table
EA Reserved Instances and Azure Savings Plans

EA customers can purchase Reserved VM Instances (1- or 3-year) and Azure Savings Plans at the enrollment level with shared scope, so the discount applies across all subscriptions in the enrollment automatically. This contrasts with PAYG, where reservations must be scoped to specific subscriptions.

# List all reservations in the EA enrollment
az reservations reservation-order list \
  --query "[].{Name:name,Term:properties.term,Status:properties.provisioningState,SKU:properties.reservedResourceType}" \
  -o table

# Purchase a 3-year reserved instance at enrollment scope (shared)
az reservations reservation-order purchase \
  --reservation-order-id "<new-guid>" \
  --sku "Standard_D8s_v5" \
  --location "eastus" \
  --reserved-resource-type VirtualMachines \
  --billing-scope "/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/<billing-account-id>" \
  --term P3Y \
  --billing-plan Upfront \
  --quantity 10 \
  --display-name "d8sv5-shared-eastus-3yr"

# Check reservation utilisation across the enrollment
az consumption reservations summaries list \
  --grain Monthly \
  --reservation-order-id "<order-id>" \
  --query "[].{Month:usageDate,Used:usedHours,Reserved:reservedHours,Util:avgUtilizationPercentage}" \
  -o table
EA vs MCA vs PAYG: Comparison
Negotiated or standard with MCA discount
Billing Account > Profile > Invoice Section > Subscription
Large enterprises with predictable Azure spend
EA Subscription Governance with Azure Policy

EA customers typically manage dozens to hundreds of subscriptions. Azure Policy applied at the management group level is the primary tool for ensuring every subscription meets baseline governance requirements automatically, without relying on per-subscription manual configuration.

# Create a policy initiative (set) for EA subscription baseline governance
cat > /tmp/ea-baseline-initiative.json << 'EOF'
{
  "displayName": "EA Subscription Baseline",
  "description": "Governance baseline applied to all EA subscriptions",
  "policyDefinitions": [
    {
      "policyDefinitionId": "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/2a0e14a6-b0a6-4fab-991a-187a4f81c498",
      "policyDefinitionReferenceId": "AllowedLocations"
    },
    {
      "policyDefinitionId": "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025",
      "policyDefinitionReferenceId": "RequireTagEnvironment",
      "parameters": {"tagName": {"value": "Environment"}}
    },
    {
      "policyDefinitionId": "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025",
      "policyDefinitionReferenceId": "RequireTagCostCentre",
      "parameters": {"tagName": {"value": "CostCentre"}}
    }
  ]
}
EOF

az policy set-definition create \
  --name "ea-subscription-baseline" \
  --display-name "EA Subscription Baseline" \
  --definitions /tmp/ea-baseline-initiative.json

# Assign the initiative to the root management group (applies to all subscriptions)
az policy assignment create \
  --name "ea-baseline" \
  --policy-set-definition "ea-subscription-baseline" \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<root-mg-id>" \
  --display-name "EA Subscription Baseline — All Subscriptions"

# Check compliance across all subscriptions
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group "<root-mg-id>" \
  --query "{Total:results.resourceDetails[0].count,Compliant:results.resourceDetails[1].count}" \
  -o table

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# configure-new-ea-sub.sh — run after provisioning a new EA subscription

SUB_ID="$1"
DEPT="$2"           # e.g. "Engineering"
COST_CENTRE="$3"    # e.g. "CC-1234"
ENV="$4"            # prod | staging | dev
MG_NAME="mg-${DEPT,,}-${ENV}"  # e.g. mg-engineering-prod

echo "=== Configuring subscription: $SUB_ID ==="

# 1. Add to management group
az account management-group subscription add --name "$MG_NAME" --subscription "$SUB_ID"
echo "Added to management group: $MG_NAME"

# 2. Create standard resource groups with cost tags
for REGION in eastus westeurope; do
  az group create \
    --name "rg-${ENV}-${REGION}" \
    --location "$REGION" \
    --subscription "$SUB_ID" \
    --tags Department="$DEPT" CostCentre="$COST_CENTRE" Environment="$ENV"
  echo "Created resource group: rg-${ENV}-${REGION}"
done

# 3. Set budget at subscription level
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "${ENV}-monthly-budget" \
  --amount 2000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date "$(date +%Y-%m-01)" \
  --end-date "2027-12-31" \
  --subscription "$SUB_ID" \
  --notifications '{
    "actual_80": {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":80,"contactEmails":["ops@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"},
    "actual_100": {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":100,"contactEmails":["ops@example.com","finance@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"}
  }'
echo "Budget created."

# 4. Assign Cost Management Reader to finance team
az role assignment create \
  --role "Cost Management Reader" \
  --assignee "finance@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID" \
  --subscription "$SUB_ID"

echo "=== Configuration complete for $SUB_ID ==="
EA Migration to Microsoft Customer Agreement (MCA)

Microsoft has been encouraging EA customers to transition to Microsoft Customer Agreement (MCA) when their EA term expires. MCA offers a similar level of discount with a more modern billing hierarchy and no minimum spend requirement. The migration transfers subscriptions but does not move resources — Azure resources continue running throughout.
EA terms are 3 years; MCA is continuous with monthly invoice cadence.
MCA supports programmatic subscription creation via the Azure portal and Bicep (similar to EA).
MCA billing hierarchy: Billing Account → Billing Profile → Invoice Section → Subscription.
The transition is initiated through the Microsoft account team when the EA approaches renewal.
After migration, ea.azure.com is replaced by the Azure portal's Cost Management + Billing section.

# After MCA migration: verify the new billing account type
az billing account list \
  --query "[].{Name:displayName,Type:agreementType,Status:accountStatus}" -o table

# List billing profiles in the MCA account
az billing profile list \
  --account-name "<mca-billing-account-id>" \
  --query "[].{Profile:displayName,Currency:currency,Status:status}" -o table

# List invoice sections under a billing profile
az billing invoice section list \
  --account-name "<mca-billing-account-id>" \
  --profile-name "<billing-profile-id>" \
  --query "[].{Section:displayName,Status:status}" -o table

# Confirm subscriptions now appear under MCA
az billing subscription list \
  --account-name "<mca-billing-account-id>" \
  --profile-name "<billing-profile-id>" \
  --query "[].{Sub:displayName,Id:subscriptionId,Status:subscriptionBillingStatus}" \
  -o table
Checking EA vs MCA Billing API Differences

# EA: query enrollment-level costs
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/<ea-billing-account>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"Department"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

# MCA: query billing-profile-level costs (replaces EA department)
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/<mca-account>/billingProfiles/<profile>" \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"InvoiceSection"}]' \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  -o table

EA includes a special subscription offer type called Azure Dev/Test, available to organisations with active Visual Studio subscriptions. Dev/Test subscriptions receive deeply discounted rates on many resource types (Windows VMs at Linux prices, SQL Database at reduced rates) in exchange for a restriction: no SLA, and resources must be used exclusively for development and testing workloads.

# Create a Dev/Test subscription under an EA enrollment account
az account create \
  --display-name "eng-dev-eastus" \
  --offer-type MS-AZR-0148P \
  --enrollment-account-object-id "<enrollment-account-object-id>" \
  --billing-account-name "<billing-account-name>"
# MS-AZR-0148P is the EA Dev/Test offer type
# MS-AZR-0017P is the standard EA Production offer type

# After creation, confirm the offer type
az account show --subscription "eng-dev-eastus" \
  --query "subscriptionPolicies.quotaId" -o tsv
# Should return: EnterpriseAgreement_2014-09-01 or EnterpriseAgreementDev_2014-09-01

# Apply a deny policy for non-dev resource types on Dev/Test subscriptions
az policy assignment create \
  --name "devtest-deny-production-skus" \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/cccc23c7-8427-4f53-ad12-b6a63eb452b3 \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<devtest-sub-id>" \
  --params '{
    "listOfAllowedSKUs": {
      "value": ["Standard_B2s","Standard_B4ms","Standard_D2s_v5","Standard_D4s_v5"]
    }
  }'

An Enterprise Agreement is a 3-year volume licensing contract that gives large organisations discounted Azure pricing, a structured billing hierarchy (Enrollment → Department → EA Account → Subscription), and an Azure Prepayment balance that resources draw down against. Enterprise Administrators have full visibility and control at the enrollment level; Department Administrators and Account Owners have narrower scopes. Subscriptions are created programmatically under EA Accounts and should follow a naming convention from day one. Cost exports, reserved instances with shared enrollment scope, and the Cost Management APIs give EA customers rich tooling for financial governance at scale.
